title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Field Notes (Published: September 30, 2001) BIRDING Eagle River bird hike to be held for free Search for fall birds on a guided hike sponsored by the Eagle River Nature Center and Anchorage Audubon Society at 10 a.m. Saturday at the center. On a short hike around nearby trails at the center, you may see black-backed and three-toed woodpeckers, goshawks, dippers, and fall migrants. The bird hike is free; parking is $5 per vehicle. For more information, call Anchorage Audubon field trip chairman John Lofgreen at 694-0510. KIDS Youth walk to reveal moose habits Junior naturalists can learn about Alaska's majestic moose at the Eagle River Nature Center at 2 p.m. on Saturday. A staff naturalist will take the youngsters on a short walk on the Rodak Trail to investigate different signs left…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Silvers sell fast in victims benefit SALMON: Profits will go to families of fallen New York firefighters. By Zaz Hollander Anchorage Daily News (Published: September 30, 2001) Alaskans turned out in droves Saturday morning to help terrorist attack victims by filling freezers with Copper River silver salmon. Hundreds of people toting coolers and sleepy kids lined up in parking lots at three area Carrs grocery stores for salmon sales to benefit the families of fallen New York firefighters. The frozen silvers sold out at Muldoon Road and Wasilla Carrs stores by 11 a.m. and at the Huffman Road store within half an hour after that. "It worked out great for us because we didn't get to go fishing this year. And we get to help out," said Ann Strother, an Alaska resident since 1969 who waited…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Inside Alaska Business (Published: September 29, 2001) Russian businessmen to visit city A group of businessmen from the major Russian Far East port city of Vladivostok are coming to Anchorage for a month to see how American businesses work, say officials at the American Russian Center on the University of Alaska Anchorage campus. The 10 businessmen, who will intern with local companies, will be here from Nov. 10 to Dec. 7. They are involved in industries like heavy machinery sales, advertising and publishing, shipping, insurance, hotel management and cellular phones. All know English. The visit is part of the nonprofit center's participation in Community Connections, a U.S. State Department effort to foster democracy and free-market business skills in Russia. The visitors need host …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | No health risk seen at Red Dog TOXINS: Early tests suggest local berries, greens are OK to eat. By Paula Dobbyn Anchorage Daily News (Published: September 29, 2001) Preliminary results from testing berries and greens about a mile from the Red Dog mine port suggest locals are not risking their health by gathering wild foods there, state officials said this week. Although it's too early to draw conclusions, the information collected so far indicates only trace amounts of lead and zinc were detected in the samples, said Ron Klein, the Alaska Department of Environmental Conservation's Red Dog project manager. "Nothing I've seen or heard indicates an imminent public health risk. It just doesn't appear to be there," Klein said. Villagers from nearby Kivalina have traditionally picked salmo…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Crew actions in ship fire criticized NIEUW AMSTERDAM: Cruise liner blaze was not properly fought, NTSB finds. By Dan Joling The Associated Press (Published: September 29, 2001) When a crewman's hot water kettle set fire to a cabin on board the Holland America Line cruise ship Nieuw Amsterdam 16 months ago in Glacier Bay, officers and crew members rushed to take action. According to the National Transportation Safety Board, much of it was the wrong action. In a report released this week, the NTSB said officers ignored their own shipboard firefighting plan. As a result, fire spread beyond the cabin, smoke reached upper decks and contact with the Coast Guard was not made for nearly an hour. "Had several officers handled the emergency differently, the fire might never have escaped from t…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Local officials mull over election move FALL: Some say campaigning for spring is too difficult. By Katie Pesznecker Anchorage Daily News (Published: September 28, 2001) The Anchorage Assembly and the Anchorage School Board are considering moving local elections back to the fall. Spring municipal elections debuted in 1993 in hopes of boosting voter turnout, said Dick Traini, Assembly chairman. "I would dare say it's done the exact opposite," he said Thursday in a joint meeting between the Assembly and the board. The Assembly had figured that local issues and candidates were getting lost in the noise of state and national campaigns during the fall elections. Now, however, some Assembly members are saying that fall elections make better sense. For one thing, it is a lot easier to campai…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Inside Alaska Business (Published: September 27, 2001) Mountain View gets new restaurant La Casita del Mar, a Mexican, American and seafood restaurant, opened at 4133 Mountain View Drive, Anchorage. Hours are 7 a.m. to 10 p.m. Monday through Friday, 11 a.m. to 10 p.m. Saturday and 11 a.m. to 9 p.m. Sunday. Phone is 279-9712. Allstate agent opens Wasilla office Allstate agent Michael Hughes opened an agency at 1365 E. Parks Highway, Suite 100, Wasilla. He previously was the president of Home First Financial, Allstate said, and has two years of insurance experience and 16 years of mortgage industry experience. Workers' comp insurer starts up Private workers' compensation insurer Liberty Northwest Insurance Corp. opened an office at the Key Bank Building in Anchorage, 101 W. Benson Blvd…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | 9th Circuit denies Greenpeace challenge to Northstar BEAUFORT SEA: Offshore oil field's environmental statement sufficient. By Maureen Clark The Associated Press (Published: September 27, 2001) The 9th U.S. Circuit Court of Appeals has denied a challenge to the development of the Northstar oil field in the Beaufort Sea. The environmental group Greenpeace and a group of North Slope Natives had argued that the Environmental Impact Statement for the project approved by the Minerals Management Service was inadequate. The group also said BP Exploration (Alaska) Inc. did not have an adequate oil spill response plan for the project. In a ruling handed down Wednesday, the court said that the Environmental Impact Statement "reasonably documented the environmental effects of Northstar." The th…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Local Folks (Published: September 26, 2001) Heather R. Whitney, a sophomore at Colorado State University in Fort Collins, received a $1,000 Eleanor Westfall Holmes Scholarship from the College of Natural Sciences for the 2001-02 school year. Whitney, a chemistry major, is a 2000 Service High School graduate. She has also received the Chemical Rubber Co. Press Freshman Chemistry Achievement Award. Student winners of the 2001 Toshiba/NSTA ExploraVision Awards program -- one of the world's largest K-12 science and technology competitions -- attended an awards celebration in Washington, D.C., earlier this year. A group from Rogers Park Elementary School placed first in Grades 4-6. Students were: Shayna Joy Cott, Travis Gingerich, Isaac Park and Grahm Nelson. Michelle Johansen was coach, …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Community Profile (Published: September 21, 2001) THORNE BAY POPULATION: 557 (2000) LOCATION: East coast of Prince of Wales Island, 47 air miles northwest of Ketchikan, 60 miles from Hollis, and 36 miles east of Klawock Junction. DESCRIPTION: Evolving from a work camp, Thorne Bay has become a year-round home to many logging employees, more than 95 percent of them non-Native. Employment is related primarily to the logging industry and federal management of the Tongass National Forest, with some commercial fishing (24 residents hold permits), tourism and government employment. To supplement income, residents fish and trap. Thorne Bay is a second-class city that levies a 3 percent sales tax. Source: Alaska Dept. of Community and Economic Development www.dced.state.ak.us/mra/CF_COMDB.htm…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Students round up fish sense FIELD TRIP: Annual Campbell Creek event offers salmon insight. By Katie Pesznecker Anchorage Daily News (Published: September 21, 2001) Dozens of squealing students and their parents splashed into a sun-freckled bend of Campbell Creek on Thursday morning with one goal: to corral some cohos. The salmon roundup, an annual event, gives kids a biology lesson and gives the state Department of Fish and Game eggs to grow more fish. Nearly 2,000 Anchorage-area students participated this week, netting fish and taking fertilized eggs back to their classrooms to grow and hatch. The students will release the silver salmon smolt this spring at Taku-Campbell Lake. "Oh, I bet they're cold," said Colette Moring, watching from the walkway along the creek shore. "I'm glad …

title: Insurance exceptions surprising
summary: Q. I just landed a new job and was thrilled because I finally got health insurance. So I took care of some things I've needed for a long time, like braces and birth control pills and a prescription to help me lose weight. Then I found out that this health plan is totally crappy, that my employer's policy makes me pay for half of all the dental care and won't pay at all for my birth control pills and weight-loss drugs. I feel like my employer wasn't up front with me when they told me I had medical coverage and a "really good plan." Is this legal? What's the deal? A. More and more employers require that employees pay 50 percent of their health insurance costs on optional services like orthodontia. Before you get too ticked off at your employer, you need to realize what's happening. As health…

title: Kodiak's massive coho run drawing fishermen from near and far.
summary: With such great fishing around Anchorage this year, it might be easy to overlook even better fishing a short plane ride away. But the outsized silver salmon of Kodiak Island are especially abundant, so much so that they can become nuisances for anglers in search of king salmon and halibut. On a recent king salmon trip offshore, we caught silvers from the surface to the bottom in 130 feet of water. No matter what tackle we used or how we fished, we couldn't sneak a hook through the silvers to reach the kings lurking below. Alaska Department of Fish and Game biologist Len Schwarz said Kodiak's silver run appears "above average to perhaps very strong," although precise counts are difficult because few Kodiak rivers and streams have weirs. Still, there are promising signs. "In Pauls Bay," he s…

title: Program gets funding for care for seniors
summary: Program gets funding for care for seniors The Mabel T. Caverly Senior Center has received a second year of funding for the DEAP Program, which offers qualifying individuals $300 micro-grants to use as reimbursement for the cost of dental work, eyeglasses, hearing aids, or prescription drugs. Applicants must be 55 or older and live in the Municipality of Anchorage; single person's income not to exceed $21,000 per year; married couple's income not to exceed $24,000 per year. Variances considered for extraordinary circumstances. For more information or to apply, call the Mabel T. Caverly Senior Center, 276-1496. Recipients of proceeds from ball named The following organizations have been named recipients of proceeds from the upcoming Mayor's Charity Ball: Alzheimer's Resource of Alaska, Ameri…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Community profile (Published: September 19, 2001) Seldovia Population: 286 (2000) LOCATION: On the Kenai Peninsula, on the south shore of Kachemak Bay, across from Homer via a 15-minute flight and a 45-minute flight from Anchorage. DESCRIPTION: A commercial fishing and processing center; 62 residents hold commercial permits, and shellfish is the prime catch. Subsistence is also an integral part of the local culture; nearly a quarter of the residents are wholly or partly Alaska Native. Timber operations at Jakolof Bay and Seldovia Bay contribute to the local economy, and tourism is increasing. The one school is attended by 80 students. Seldovia is a first-class city incorporated within the Kenai Peninsula Borough, and it levies a 3 percent sales tax. HISTORY: Native residents are mixe…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Alaska Briefs (Published: September 19, 2001) Timber interests laud ruling on Tongass JUNEAU -- A timber group is applauding an appeals court ruling that blocks efforts to reinstate a ban on logging in parts of the Tongass National Forest. A three-judge panel of the Ninth Circuit Court of Appeals in San Francisco ruled Monday that the Sierra Club and other environmental groups could not appeal a May 23 decision by U.S. District Judge James Singleton. That judge had lifted his own ban on logging in roadless areas. In March, Singleton ordered that none of the 9.3 million acres of roadless area in the Tongass could be altered. That stopped 67 timber sales on 11,500 acres of the forest. Two pipelines make sense, Foothills says WHITEHORSE, Yukon -- It would be cheaper to build two separat…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Helping hands Alaskans find wide range of ways to contribute to relief efforts By Lisa Demer Anchorage Daily News (Published: September 19, 2001) Breanna Lango filled a laundry hamper with her toys and stuffed animals Saturday, intending to sell them and give the money to people affected by the Sept. 11 terrorist attacks. Her big sister Megan suggested that a lemonade stand might get more customers. Now, with the help of their moms, the sisters and other kids in their Independence Park neighborhood aim to raise $500, one brownie at a time, for the American Red Cross. "It's better than just thinking it is sad. We are really doing something to help," Breanna, 8, said Tuesday afternoon while her mother, Dana, took cinnamon rolls out of the oven and put in another batch of brownies. Afte…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | FISHING REPORT: Fall means the fish are getting hungry EDITOR'S NOTE: This is the last weekly fishing report of the year. However, the Sunday Outdoors section will continue to appear year-round. By Ken Marsh Anchorage Daily News (Published: September 14, 2001) Autumn in Alaska starts up high, as a blush in the Alpine reaches that bleeds down with the cooling days to poplar and birch forests below. In that sense, this is a season in motion. And as the trees light up in vibrant yellow hues, the waters of Southcentral grow dark and cold. Some of the year's best fishing is set to begin. The thing to do, if only there was time, would be to travel with the changing seasons. A perfect fall fishing journey might start north of the Arctic Circle -- say along the Kobuk River, where sheefish (t…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Former Alaskan on list of missing PENTAGON: Navy man who attended UAF wanted to be near kin. The Associated Press Wire (Published: September 17, 2001) Fairbanks -- A Navy enlisted man who grew up in Alaska and chose to work in the Pentagon to be closer to his family is among the people missing after a commercial jet hijacked by terrorists struck the building Tuesday. Petty Officer 2nd class Ronald Hemenway, 37, hasn't been heard from since the attack, said his parents, Bob and Shirley Hemenway of Shawnee, Kan. Hemenway's name is on a list of 188 people released by the Defense Department. He attended the University of Alaska Fairbanks. He has a wife, Marinella; and two children, Stefan, 3, and Desiree, 1. They live at Bolling Air Force Base near Washington. Shirley Hemenway told the W…

title: FISHING REPORT: Fall means the fish are getting hungry
summary: Autumn in Alaska starts up high, as a blush in the Alpine reaches that bleeds down with the cooling days to poplar and birch forests below. In that sense, this is a season in motion. And as the trees light up in vibrant yellow hues, the waters of Southcentral grow dark and cold. Some of the year's best fishing is set to begin. The thing to do, if only there was time, would be to travel with the changing seasons. A perfect fall fishing journey might start north of the Arctic Circle -- say along the Kobuk River, where sheefish (they resemble tarpon and fight just as hard) grow to 50 pounds or better. From there you could move southward on autumn's crest to the lakes and sloughs around Minto, near Fairbanks, where northern pike as long as men's legs lurk among the lily pads. Beyond that, gray…

title: 15-year-old North Pole girl spots, shoots big caribou
summary: Chrissy Seay was sitting on a four-wheeler eating a sandwich and talking with Ryan, her little brother, when the rocks she had been looking at a half-mile away got up and started walking. "The sun hit them and the rocks started moving," said the 15-year-old Seay. "I said, There's two caribou over there.' " Her mother, Page, said her daughter was a little more enthusiastic than she lets on. "She started jumping up and down, yelling, There's two caribou! There's two caribou!' " Page Seay said. Chrissy, a sophomore at North Pole High School, admits she might have gotten a little excited, but she had reason to. It marked the first time in her three year-hunting career that she was the one who spotted game. "I've never spotted any caribou that we've been able to shoot," she said. Chrissy and he…

title: ANCHORAGEMammoth Music building suffers minor damage in afternoon blaze
summary: ANCHORAGE Mammoth Music building suffers minor damage in afternoon blaze A fire Saturday afternoon melted some of the selections at the Mammoth Music at Spenard Road and 29th Avenue. Eight Anchorage Fire Department units responded to the fire, according to Anchorage fire Capt. Dan Joyner. The cause of the fire remains under investigation. Store owner Forrest Jackson -- a tenant of the cinder block building -- said he has fire insurance but doesn't yet know the extent of the damage. A lot of the music and T-shirts inside were damaged, according to Joyner. The building sustained minor damage; firefighters broke the plate glass windows in front of the store to give heavy smoke an escape route. -- Anchorage Daily News ANCHORAGE Man charged with attempting to kill another man with pickup truck …

title: New rules to protect Steller sea lions could hurt fishing industry
summary: Sitka -- A federal panel is considering new fishing restrictions in the nation's largest pollock fishery in an effort to protect dwindling numbers of Steller sea lions. Sweeping new rules on pollock, Pacific cod and Atka mackerel fisheries under consideration by the North Pacific Fishery Management Council could greatly increase the level of regulatory oversight on Bering Sea and Gulf of Alaska fishing, a spokesman said. "It completely changes the face and nature of pollock, mackerel and Pacific cod fisheries," said Dave Witherell, spokesman for the council. Of five options being considered, one plan favored by National Marine Fishery Service would further shift some fishing away from sea lion rookeries and hunting areas at an estimated cost of up to 513 jobs in the Western Alaska fishing …

title: Ready For Kindergarten
summary: Nearly 3,300 Anchorage kindergartners and their parents are in schools this week checking out their new classrooms while being checked out by their new teachers. This is the second year the district has done developmental profiles -- conferences between the teacher, new kindergarten students and their parents. During the meeting, parents talk about their children's personalities: Are they are shy or bossy? How long is their attention span? Educators say one of the best parts of the session is that children get to scope out their new classrooms, seeing where they will hang their coats, eat their lunches and have recess.

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Shooting linked to road rage ARRESTS: Police apprehend two suspects in Friday morning incident. By Lucas Wall Anchorage Daily News (Published: September 15, 2001) Anchorage police are investigating a shooting Friday morning on Fourth Avenue downtown that appears to have been sparked by road rage. Sgt. Ron Spohnholz was driving to work about 7:20 a.m. when a 25-year-old man driving a gray Chevrolet Camaro flagged him down at Fourth Avenue and K Street. The man told Spohnholz he had been shot at by an occupant of a red Honda. Spohnholz found the Honda three blocks away at Seventh Avenue and arrested Louis Tavares, 23, and Christopher Sullivan, 24. Police said Sullivan was driving the Honda and Tavares was a passenger. It's unknown whether the shot came from the passenger or the driver.…

summary: | | | Prayers Take Flight Girl Scouts remember victims during anniversary celebration By Zaz Hollander Anchorage Daily News (Published: September 16, 2001) Leola Rutherford of Girdwood shows her old badges to Girl Scout Troop 422 leader Kris Miller of Chugiak on Saturday at Kincaid Park. (Photo by Erik Hill / Anchorage Daily News) Scouts, from left, Katie Sanders, Maggie Hepler, Alex Lauer and Carol Hepler show how teamwork can get things done Saturday at the trolley activity station. (Photo by Erik Hill / Anchorage Daily News) Fifty years ago, 900 Girl Scouts in Southcentral Alaska formed the Susitna Council. The United States flourished in the wake of World War II. Now 5,500 Scouts strong, the council on Saturday celebrated its 50th anniversary and prayed for a country on the brink of a …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Killer vigilante gets 75 years MURDER: Samuel Camanga pleads no contest to shooting alleged child molester. By Molly Brown Anchorage Daily News (Published: September 11, 2001) An Anchorage man accused of shooting a teenager in the back of the head two years ago pleaded no contest to first-degree murder Monday and agreed to serve 75 years in prison. Samuel Jay Camanga, 22, is one of four people who police say conspired to kill Morgan "Wolfie" Gorsche. One or more of the young men believed Gorsche had sexually molested a 4-year-old girl, police said. Camanga; Paul Munson, now 19; Shane Clapper, now 20; and an unnamed juvenile picked up Gorsche, 17, at his house and drove to Eklutna Lake Road where Camanga shot Gorsche on Sept. 1, 1999. The unnamed juvenile, who police say offered to pa…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Inside Alaska Business (Published: September 11, 2001) Study tracks state fair trash Green Star Inc. assessed trash habits at the Alaska State Fair in Palmer this year in hopes of making next year's fair more environmentally friendly. "What we learned is that between one-quarter and one-half of all the waste generated could easily be recycled," Green Star said in a recent newsletter. "This recyclable portion is composed of about half corrugated cardboard and half beverage container. Almost all of the vendors at the fair bring their products to the event in cardboard boxes. "Approximately 85 percent of the fair's food vendors serve their beverages in bottles or cans rather than in paper or plastic cups," the agency said. "This means there are a lot of aluminum cans, plastic bottles an…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Alaska Digest (Published: September 14, 2001) WILLOW Two cabins apparently set afire, troopers say Two cabins in the Caswell Lakes area of Willow were apparently set on fire this week, according to Alaska State Troopers. The first report came in Tuesday. Patrick Cedros, 45, of Anchorage told troopers in Talkeetna that he found his cabin destroyed by fire. Cedros told them there were no heat sources in the structure and it had been unoccupied since Labor Day weekend. The loss is estimated at $60,000. Troopers and the State Fire Marshal's Office are investigating. Wednesday, Steven Heinrich, 42, of Willow called troopers at 2:55 a.m. to report that his cabin was on fire and he suspected arson. Damage is estimated at $10,000 and an investigation is under way. -- Anchorage Daily News BET…

title: DEBATE: As Teamsters turn up heat, head count hints drilling may pass committee but not full Senate.
summary: Washington -- The fate of drilling in the Arctic National Wildlife Refuge rests with the U.S. Senate, but the debate these days is on the airwaves, not in hearing rooms. The Teamsters union, emerging as the most prominent pro-drilling group, is about to launch its own ads to counter a recent anti-drilling advertising campaign, said Alaska Teamster leader Jerry Hood. The union's radio spots will thank specific House members who voted last month in favor of drilling, Hood said. They'll play in the representative's home district, praising some of the same Congress members environmental groups bashed in their August ads. The Teamsters are planning to launch a more ambitious ad effort after coordinating with Arctic Power and other groups advocating for drilling, Hood said. "We want to see what …

title: Man seeks new dock to launch tour boats
summary: An Anchorage man proposes to extend the city's boat launching ramp into Knik Arm and build a removable floating dock to accommodate a new summer tour-boat business. Jerry Norman, owner of Cook Inlet Excursions, wants to take tourists on two-hour sightseeing trips up and down the Arm, then out near Fire Island, he said. One hundred or more passengers would travel on a large catamaran, eat lunch and watch beluga whales. Norman said his business would try to succeed where others have failed. Norman's proposal, under review by state and federal agencies, would extend the existing ramp at Ship Creek Point by 250 feet, according to public documents. Like the city's ramp, the new ramp would be 8 feet wide. It would float on 20-foot-long pontoons, Norman said. At their extreme, tides in Knik Arm s…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Guard looks to Bush for growth RURAL: Concern about shrinking numbers leads to program aimed at villages. By Lucas Wall Anchorage Daily News (Published: September 10, 2001) Alakanuk -- The Alaska Army National Guard is reaching out to rural Alaska in an effort to win and keep troops and strengthen villages. The guard has been shrinking since the end of the Cold War, particularly in its number of Alaska Native soldiers. And a new Rural Affairs Program aims to reverse that trend. "The strongest, most consistent members of the Guard were the Alaska Native population," said Lt. Noel Pediangco, one of two Guard members working full-time on the program. "About 40 percent of the Guard was Alaska Native at some point. Now the number's closer to 30 percent." While that's still double the perc…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Former timber manager gets top Alaska Forest Service job BRINK: It's like having a vampire managing the blood bank,' environmentalist says. By Paula Dobbyn Anchorage Daily News (Published: September 8, 2001) Forest Service actions in Alaska this week could bode well for the state's beleaguered timber industry. But tough days lie ahead for forest protection advocates in the Tongass, environmentalists in Southeast Alaska said Friday. This week the Forest Service said that Steve Brink would be taking over the top agency job in Alaska on Monday. The Southeast Alaska Conservation Council called Brink a "timber beast," while a loggers' association described him as fair-minded and experienced. Brink, who has been named acting regional director, has a history in the 17 million-acre temperate…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Ready For Kindergarten (Published: September 7, 2001) Teacher Mara Gano watches her future student Brooke Davis walk a straight line while evaluating her gross motor development skills Thursday at Scenic Park. (Photo by Erik Hill / Anchorage Daily News) Nearly 3,300 Anchorage kindergartners and their parents are in schools this week checking out their new classrooms while being checked out by their new teachers. This is the second year the district has done developmental profiles -- conferences between the teacher, new kindergarten students and their parents. During the meeting, parents talk about their children's personalities: Are they are shy or bossy? How long is their attention span? Educators say one of the best parts of the session is that children get to scope out their new c…

title: Fish processors face new waste rules
summary: Belching, underwater volcanoes of rotting fish waste in Alaska caught the scrutiny of federal regulators who rolled out new rules last week to crack down on the smelly nuisances. To the relief of many Ketchikan residents who suffer through periods of fishy stench, the U.S. Environmental Protection issued tougher regulations on seafood processing plants and the waste they dump into the sea. The rules affect some 250 plants statewide, including five clustered in Ketchikan, a mountainous, blue-collar town surrounded by narrow waterways and tiny islands in Southeast. The rules affect onshore, near-shore and offshore plants, such as factory trawlers. They follow an EPA enforcement push against Alaska fish plants that has resulted in five cannery owners facing $682,000 in fines for alleged pollu…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Water works Lagoon proves harsh critic of artistic symbolism By Doug O'harra Anchorage Daily News (Published: July 30, 2001) Artist Jim Dault sets his "Bird in a Box" afloat. (Jim Lavrakas / Anchorage Daily News) Shaped vaguely like half of a light bulb, the great plastic dome launched from the dock at Westchester Lagoon with a wobble. As it keeled to one side, it threatened to dump its symbolic cargo: a miniature marine mammal, seven plastic riflemen and a tall figure suggestive of Pocahontas. "This is the actual visualization of subsistence," declared artist Jim Dault, snatching the contraption just before it capsized. "But it's tentative. It's very tentative." Indeed. On its second voyage, the sculpture flipped, allowing the visualization of traditional marine prey to sink to the …

title: Author offers tips to accumulating wealth
summary: Looking to get rich? You won't find the way to wealth in a common education or high-paying job, says Robert Kiyosaki. But you will find it in some simple precepts of wealth and money, such as building assets that put money in your pocket, harnessing money to work for you and diminishing your liabilities. It's a simple game, says Kiyosaki, author of the best-selling personal finance book, "Rich Dad, Poor Dad." Kiyosaki will speak at 7 p.m. Friday at the Egan Center in downtown Anchorage. His philosophy is based on some simple advice: "The poor and middle class work for money. The rich have money work for them." Kiyosaki is a fourth generation Japanese-American, who grew up on the big island of Hawaii. Kiyosaki's "real father" was a government official who counseled him to get good grades an…

title: Inside Alaska Business
summary: Village tests solar-diesel efficiency Lime Village, a town of 50 people, is the site of a hybrid solar and diesel-fuel generation system in a test of how well solar power can work to reduce high rural Alaska power bills, according to BP Alaska. The test is a joint effort of the oil company, the Alaska Energy Authority, McGrath Light & Power Co., the U.S. Department of Energy and the U.S. Department of Housing & Urban Development. The new system began operating in July and includes installation of 106 solar panels made by BP. A battery bank should store surplus power. It's hoped each panel will save 10 gallons of diesel annually over 20 years. During the first month, the village's diesel costs fell more than $2,000, said Phil Graham, village planner. Travel to Chicago gets easier Alaska Air…

title: Work adds to Chugiak crowding
summary: On the brink of her senior year at Chugiak High School, Jessie Malecha may have to forgo one of the small pleasures of high school supremacy. Malecha, 17, and her buddies may not be able to lounge by their lockers between classes. Two corridors will be closed for at least the first six weeks of classes because of ongoing classroom renovations. The closures only make life worse for Chugiak students and staff who work around clogged conditions daily. When halls closed last year, it could take three minutes just to squeeze up one of the stairways connecting first and second floor, Malecha said. No question there will be inconvenience, but there is no truth to rumors the school might open as late as October, said Carol Comeau, Anchorage School District superintendent. Chugiak is expected to op…

title: Waivers for educators granted
summary: Nine Anchorage teachers yanked from their classrooms last year and forced to teach special education will return to regular teaching this September. The state is granting emergency waivers to teachers who want to teach special education but haven't quite finished their certification requirements, said Jerry Sjolander, Anchorage School District executive director of special education. This will allow the district to fill all of its openings, and no one will be forced to teach special education who doesn't want to, Sjolander said. More than 30 of the district's 700 special-education positions were still vacant Friday, including 12 at the elementary level. The number is a little lower than last year, Sjolander said, but it's still too high and reflects a state teacher shortage that has made f…

title: DOT targets roadside memorials
summary: Fairbanks -- The state Department of Transportation is cracking down on roadside memorials put up by the friends and families of people killed on Alaska's highways. The agency says the memorials can distract drivers, and it has come up with an alternative program. The agency is offering white and blue signs that will cost families $500 to order and maintain for two years. After that, the DOT will take the sign down. State law prohibits any kind of advertising signs, displays or devices in the rights of way of primary and secondary roads. "Over the past couple of years we occasionally have had a complaint," said John Bennett, the department's Northern Region right-of-way chief. "For some reason they don't think it's right. They've either got a problem with a religious item or with it being …

title: State sets king crab quota
summary: The state's biggest commercial king crab fishery, anemic in recent years, will be even weaker this October when some 240 fishing boats light out into the Bering Sea. The Alaska Department of Fish and Game set this year's Bristol Bay red king crab quota at 7.2 million pounds. That's about half the amount caught in 1998 but certainly more than in 1994 and 1995, when the fishery was closed altogether because of a dip in the crab population. The giant, orange spiders are one of Alaska's most coveted seafood delicacies, usually commanding menu-popping prices in fine restaurants. Forrest Bowers, a state management biologist in Dutch Harbor, said the declining commercial quota is not a sign that red king crabs are suffering biologically. Rather, biologists believe there are fewer crabs available …

title: Russians to seize bandit fishing boat
summary: Juneau -- Russian authorities say they'll seize a fishing vessel and file criminal charges against the master after it was spotted fishing in the U.S. zone and refused orders to halt, the Coast Guard reported. Russian authorities boarded the vessel Friday and found evidence someone had tampered with the logs, said Petty Officer Christopher Grisafe of the Coast Guard. The 345-foot Kapitan Maslovets was spotted more than half a mile inside the U.S. exclusive economic zone Tuesday afternoon in an area about 990 miles west of Anchorage. A Coast Guard helicopter crew watched and videotaped as the crew hauled back its nets filled with fish. But the ship didn't respond to the helicopter crew's call to wait for a Coast Guard boarding team. The ship instead steamed out of the U.S. zone and into int…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | IN THE FIELD: The art of the stalk Hunters use variety of techniques to track down and bag big game By Ken Marsh Daily News Correspondent (Published: August 20, 2001) A hunter scans the country around Upper Paradise Lake. Hunters in Alaska use a variety of methods to find big-game animals. Three popular methods are spot-and-stalk, still hunting and calling. (Photo by KEN MARSH) Big-game hunting in Alaska is a science involving planning, strategy and refined backcountry skills. Ultimately, however, the heart of the hunt, the part that defines its success or failure, is a hunter's ability to find and get within range of the game. Generally speaking, big-game hunting methods are dictated by the terrain and species a hunter faces. Three hunting options most popular in Alaska are spot-and…

title: Tesoro drops liquor request
summary: Midtown residents and businesses won a two-year battle this week when Tesoro 2 Go withdrew a liquor license application for its gas station at Northern Lights Boulevard and A Street. Public opposition was too strong, Tesoro attorney Dan Coffey said in a letter to the city and the state Alcoholic Beverage Control Board. "I was very delighted," said Anchorage Assembly member Fay Von Gemmingen, who represents the area. She said the fight is over, but others, including Tesoro, say that's unlikely. Bad memories of the drunks drawn by an old liquor store in that area soured residents to the idea of a new liquor store, no matter what conditions Tesoro agreed to. Patrons of the store were unruly, businesses found human feces on their grounds, and kids on their way to school were scared by inebriat…

title: The Anchorage Daily News (www.adn.com)
summary: Ivanof Bay POPULATION: 22 (2000) LOCATION: On the northeast end of the Kupreanof Peninsula, 500 miles southwest of Anchorage and 250 miles southeast of Dillingham. DESCRIPTION: A community with traditional Unangan influences, where more than 95 percent of the population is partly or wholly Alaska Native. The residents practice a subsistence lifestyle, hunting and fishing for salmon, trout, crab, clams, moose, caribou, bear, porcupine and seals. Most residents work in the commercial fishing industry, with two holding commercial permits for salmon and halibut. Many trap in the winter. Water is supplied by a well and nearby stream and stored in a 20,000-gallon tank. HISTORY: The bay was named in 1880 by Lt. Dall of the U.S. Coast and Geodetic Survey. A salmon cannery operated at this site fro…

title: FISHING REPORT: Silvers are running loose all over Southcentral
summary: On Sunday, the Deshka River was a silver salmon angler's dream. From the river's languid mouth to its dashing runs several miles upstream of the Alaska Department of Fish and Game fish-counting weir, bright salmon rolled. Anglers were few. Southcentral Alaska is awash in silver salmon, the fish de jour of August. From streams like the Susitna Valley's Deshka and Talkeetna rivers to the Kenai Peninsula's Resurrection Bay and Anchor River, these hard-fighting sportfish have arrived, and continue to arrive, en masse. Of Alaska's five species of Pacific salmon, none are more coveted than silvers, also called cohos. Bury your hook into one of these 8- to 12-pound scrappers (the biggest exceed 20 pounds), and you can expect a head-shaking, cart-wheeling battle sure to strain your arms and speed …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Turning gurry into greenbacks By Laine Welch Freelance (Published: August 18, 2001) Not using fish waste is a wasted opportunity. That's the belief of two Kodiak-based food scientists who are looking at ways to make fish gurry more valuable to the seafood industry. Right now, the tons of fish skin, bones, heads and guts produced by Kodiak's seafood processors are taken to a reduction plant where it's turned into high-quality fish meal. Kermit Reppond, a research chemist with the National Marine Fisheries Service, believes the industry is missing out on significant profit potential, both locally and globally. "We take everything and lump it together. It's like if in beef processing, they just cut out rib-eyes and T-bone steaks, and turned everything else into hamburger. There are lots…

summary: | | | State sets king crab quota 7.2 MILLION POUNDS: Industry worries about Bristol Bay fishery limit. By Wesley Loy Anchorage Daily News (Published: August 18, 2001) The state's biggest commercial king crab fishery, anemic in recent years, will be even weaker this October when some 240 fishing boats light out into the Bering Sea. The Alaska Department of Fish and Game set this year's Bristol Bay red king crab quota at 7.2 million pounds. That's about half the amount caught in 1998 but certainly more than in 1994 and 1995, when the fishery was closed altogether because of a dip in the crab population. The giant, orange spiders are one of Alaska's most coveted seafood delicacies, usually commanding menu-popping prices in fine restaurants. Forrest Bowers, a state management biologist in Dutc…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Agency launches air safety survey NIOSH: Pilots, carriers asked what can be done to prevent fatalities. By Maureen Clark The Associated Press (Published: August 14, 2001) The National Institute for Occupational Safety and Health is asking commercial pilots and air taxi operators for information that could help prevent fatal plane crashes. The agency began mailing a safety survey this month to learn more about what people in the aviation industry think could be done to prevent fatalities. Plane crashes have been the leading cause of occupational fatalities in Alaska in recent years. An Alaska pilot has a 1-in-8 chance of dying on the job during a 30-year career. That's 100 times greater than the average U.S. worker. The survey is being done as part of a three-year effort intended to r…

title: Treehouse fire prompts stabbing
summary: Anchorage firefighters responding Monday evening to a report of a wildfire in Far North Bicentennial Park instead found a burning treehouse. A 51-year-old man living there told them he assaulted the arsonist and another man. Shortly after 5 p.m., the Fire Department received a call of smoke visible in the park, south of Tudor Road near Chugach Electric Association's university substation. Firefighters summoned police when they found not a wildfire but a crime scene. Three officers responded about 5:30 and hiked about 10 minutes into the woods. They found one man who had been stabbed and a second who had been thrown into Campbell Creek. They arrested the treehouse's resident, who was sitting on a footbridge over the creek listening to a radio, officer Chris Nelson said. The incident began w…

title: KODIAKCoast Guard celebrates 112th by commissioning a cutter
summary: KODIAK Coast Guard celebrates 112th by commissioning a cutter The U.S. Coast Guard commissioned a new ship Friday in Kodiak as part of this weekend's celebrations of the Coast Guard's 211th birthday. Coast Guard Cutter Spar was commissioned into active service during a Friday morning ceremony. The cutter is named for the 10,000 women who volunteered to service in the Coast Guard during World War II. A previous cutter named Spar was decommissioned in 1997. The Coast Guard motto is semper paratus, Latin for always ready, said spokesman Roger Wetherell. The women who volunteered during World War II became known as SPARs, he said. Cutter Spar was built at Marinette Shipyard in Marinette, Wis. Lt. Cmdr. Joanna Nunan, commanding officer, and 45 crew members accepted the ship March 9. They sailed…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Renowned bowhead whale expert retires ALBERTS: After decades on Arctic coast, couple plan move to Pennsylvania farm. By Earl Finkler Daily News Correspondent (Published: August 26, 2001) Barrow -- After more than two decades of studying bowhead whales in the company of people who have lived with them for thousands of years, Tom Albert and his wife, Marnie, are retiring from their work on Alaska's Arctic coast. The couple plan to build a home on some Pennsylvania farmland. "We plan to raise groundhogs, feed the birds and just sort of watch the world go by," Albert said. Albert served as a senior scientist for the North Slope Borough's Wildlife Management Department and is recognized internationally as an expert on the bowhead whale. He worked closely with Inupiat Eskimo elders and hel…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Bongs or pipes? Judge weighs question SEIZED: Items taken as drug paraphernalia' with defective search warrant. By Molly Brown Anchorage Daily News (Published: August 18, 2001) The city wants Doug Myers' bongs. And Myers, owner of The Black Market, wants them back. Only he calls them pipes. The pipes, or bongs, were the subject of a hearing Friday in which two attorneys asked a judge to figure out if items seized with a defective search warrant can be considered contraband. Some 31 boxes of goods were taken by Anchorage police from The Black Market in March, after the Anchorage Assembly made it a criminal offense to sell "drug paraphernalia." City attorneys later determined something was wrong with the search warrant and didn't charge Myers with a criminal offense. But they also didn…

The state is granting emergency waivers to teachers who want to teach special education but haven't quite finished their certification requirements, said Jerry Sjolander, Anchorage School District executive director of special education. This will allow the district to fill all of its openings, and no one will be forced to teach special education who doesn't want to, Sjolander said. More than 30 of the district's 700 special-education positions were still vacant Friday, includin…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Hitchhiker steals vehicle to get across the border CANADA: Man accused of taking the van from motel and RV park By Lucas Wall Anchorage Daily News (Published: August 18, 2001) A 37-year-old Fairbanks man hitchhiking to Arizona is accused of stealing a van near the Alaska-Yukon border and driving it into Canada. Early one morning last week, Canadian customs inspectors notified the Royal Canadian Mounted Police that a van had driven through the Beaver Creek, Yukon, checkpoint without stopping for inspection. Danielle Deterding said she was awakened in the early morning by a trucker blaring his horn. Deterding and her husband, Neil, own Border City Motel & RV Park in Alcan Border, four miles west of Canada on the Alaska Highway. The trucker had a message from U.S. Customs stating they t…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Judge rules meeting illegal HATCHER PASS: Both sides claim victory after the decision. By S.J. Komarnitsky Anchorage Daily News (Published: August 18, 2001) Wasilla -- Mat-Su Borough officials illegally went behind closed doors and withheld important information from the public in deciding to buy a land lease to develop a ski area at Hatcher Pass, a state judge ruled Friday. Palmer Superior Court Judge Eric Smith said the borough violated the state's open meetings and public records laws. But he denied a request to rescind the decision, saying borough officials corrected those violations by holding a public hearing before voting to purchase the lease. The verdict means the borough can move forward with plans to buy the lease from Anchorage developer Greg Romack, who announced in Marc…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | DOT targets roadside memorials HIGHWAYS: State will offer alternative signs for $500. The Associated Press (Published: August 14, 2001) Fairbanks -- The state Department of Transportation is cracking down on roadside memorials put up by the friends and families of people killed on Alaska's highways. The agency says the memorials can distract drivers, and it has come up with an alternative program. The agency is offering white and blue signs that will cost families $500 to order and maintain for two years. After that, the DOT will take the sign down. State law prohibits any kind of advertising signs, displays or devices in the rights of way of primary and secondary roads. "Over the past couple of years we occasionally have had a complaint," said John Bennett, the department's Northern…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Tesoro drops liquor request MIDTOWN:Public opposition prompts gas station to withdraw alcohol license application. By Rosemary Shinohara Anchorage Daily News (Published: August 31, 2001) Midtown residents and businesses won a two-year battle this week when Tesoro 2 Go withdrew a liquor license application for its gas station at Northern Lights Boulevard and A Street. Public opposition was too strong, Tesoro attorney Dan Coffey said in a letter to the city and the state Alcoholic Beverage Control Board. "I was very delighted," said Anchorage Assembly member Fay Von Gemmingen, who represents the area. She said the fight is over, but others, including Tesoro, say that's unlikely. Bad memories of the drunks drawn by an old liquor store in that area soured residents to the idea of a new l…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Community datebook (Published: September 1, 2001) TODAY COMMUNITY Alaska State Fair, 10 a.m.-10 p.m. daily through Monday at Alaska State Fairgrounds, Palmer. Enjoy the animals, rides, foods, games and shows. Admission is $8 adults, $5 youths and seniors plus concert fees. There's also a old-timey bluegrass and square dance. Camping is free. title: The Anchorage Daily News (www.adn.com)
summary: PITKAS POINT POPULATION: 125 (2000) LOCATION: Near the junction of the Yukon and Andreafsky rivers, five miles northwest of St. Marys on the Yukon-Kuskokwim Delta, three miles by road from the Saint Marys airport. DESCRIPTION: A Yup'ik Eskimo village dependent on a subsistence lifestyle. Almost 94 percent of the village is wholly or partly Alaska Native. Employment is limited, although some year-round enterprises are available. Subsistence activities provide food sources, including salmon, moose, bear and waterfowl. Dog sledding is prevalent. One resident holds a commercial fishing permit. All supplies are brought in through Saint Marys; there are no public facilities other than a school (attended by 36 students) and washeteria. Water is supplied by a small stream and infiltration gallery.…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Inside Alaska Business (Published: September 1, 2001) Alaska Air service to D.C. to start Alaska Airlines inaugurates its Washington, D.C., service Tuesday. The flight leaves Anchorage at 7:30 a.m. with one stop, in Seattle. A ceremony expected to be attended by William Ayer, president of the Seattle-based airline, and Anchorage Mayor George Wuerch is scheduled for 6:30 a.m. Tuesday, the airline said. New entrance for Alaska Regional The main entrance of Alaska Regional Hospital in Anchorage will be closed starting Thursday due to construction, the hospital said. The emergency room entrance will not change. The hospital's new main entrance and free valet parking service will be located on the west side of the building, the opposite side from the current entrance. The new entrance wil…
